food_name: Nasi Padang (Rice with Rendang, Sayur Lodeh, and Sambal Goreng)
aliases: Nasi Padang (rice with rendang, sayur lodeh, and sambal goreng)

english: Nasi Padang (rice with rendang, sayur lodeh, and sambal goreng)

region_of_origin: West Sumatra, Indonesia; ubiquitous in Singapore hawker centres
food_subgroup: Composite rice dish with multiple side dishes

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific entry for Nasi Padang. Closest parent is a rice-based composite meal with multiple side dishes (FOODON:00002473 — rice dish). No exact match exists for the Minangkabau composite preparation.
ontology.foodex2_description: Mixed rice dish with multiple side dishes (Malay/Indonesian composite)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A00PB is the closest FoodEx2 parent for composite rice dishes; no specific Nasi Padang code exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Nasi Padang or Minangkabau composite rice dish as of 2026.

serving.description: 1 plate (approximately 400g total: ~200g steamed rice + ~80g rendang + ~70g sayur lodeh + ~50g sambal goreng)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker-centre plate of nasi padang weighs approximately 400g and comprises roughly 200g steamed jasmine rice, 80g beef rendang, 70g sayur lodeh, and 50g sambal goreng, delivering approximately 700 kcal per serving.

nutrient_source.title: Nasi Padang (composite) — HPB Singapore Foodo; cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD component data for rendang, sayur lodeh, and sambal goreng
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; Institute for Medical Research (IMR) Malaysia
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo provides composite nasi padang values for the canonical Singapore hawker combination (rice + rendang + sayur lodeh + sambal goreng). Individual component values cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD for rendang and sayur lodeh. Per-100g values represent the weighted composite of the canonical plate.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — University of Sydney GI Database; jasmine rice GI ~73 as dominant driver, composite GI reduced by fat content of rendang and coconut milk components
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for the canonical Singapore nasi padang composite. GI of 62 is estimated using jasmine rice (GI ~73, University of Sydney GI Database) as the dominant carbohydrate driver (~200g of 400g plate). The fat-rich rendang and coconut milk in sayur lodeh slow gastric emptying, reducing the composite GI to approximately 62 (medium). Glycemic load per 400g serving calculated as (62 × 75g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 46.5, rounded to 46.6 per input source. This is consistent with published composite meal GI methodology (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content — approximately 1,280mg per 400g serving (>50% of the 2,400mg/day recommended limit), derived from belacan, soy sauce, fish sauce, dried shrimp, and spice pastes across all three side dishes.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit nasi padang to once per week, request reduced sambal and sauce portions, choose sayur lodeh over rendang as the primary side dish, and avoid adding extra sambal from condiment trays to keep sodium intake below 600mg per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycemic load per serving (~46.6) driven by approximately 75g total carbohydrate from 200g jasmine rice (GI ~73); composite GI of ~62 is only modestly reduced by fat content.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should reduce the rice portion to 100g (one scoop), replace the remaining volume with additional sayur lodeh vegetables and a lean protein, and monitor postprandial blood glucose, as a standard nasi padang plate represents one of the highest glycemic-load meals commonly consumed in Singapore.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat content (5.4g per 100g; approximately 21.6g per 400g serving) from coconut milk in rendang and sayur lodeh, combined with high sodium (320mg/100g) and moderate cholesterol (38mg/100g), presents a compound cardiovascular risk profile.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit nasi padang to occasional consumption, select sayur lodeh and sambal goreng over rendang to reduce saturated fat intake, reduce rice portion size to lower overall energy density, and avoid adding extra coconut-milk-based gravies.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat from coconut milk may promote systemic inflammation; however, anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, galangal, lemongrass) in the spice bases and isoflavones from tempeh in sambal goreng provide modest offsetting benefits.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis may consume nasi padang in moderation, favouring the vegetable and tempeh components over the rendang to reduce saturated fat intake while benefiting from the anti-inflammatory spice profile.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nasi Padang is a self-service composite rice meal originating from the Minangkabau culture of West Sumatra, Indonesia, and now a cornerstone of Singapore's Malay hawker food culture. The canonical Singapore hawker combination consists of steamed jasmine rice served with beef rendang (a dry-braised coconut-spiced beef curry), sayur lodeh (a mild coconut milk vegetable curry containing cabbage, long beans, and tofu), and sambal goreng (stir-fried tempeh and tofu in a chilli-belacan sambal). Each component is prepared separately using proprietary spice pastes (rempah) containing lemongrass, galangal, turmeric, dried chilli, and shallots. The dish is energy-dense (175 kcal/100g; ~700 kcal per 400g plate), high in saturated fat from coconut milk (~21.6g per plate), and moderate-high in sodium (~1,280mg per plate) from belacan, dried shrimp, and spice pastes. The composite glycemic index is approximately 62 (medium), but the glycemic load per plate is very high (~46.6) due to the large jasmine rice portion. Clinically, nasi padang is a high-concern meal for patients with type 2 diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease. The spice base contributes modest anti-inflammatory phytochemicals (curcumin from turmeric, gingerols from galangal), and tempeh provides isoflavones and plant protein. The dish is halal, dairy-free, and gluten-free in its traditional preparation.
common_user_queries: nasi padang vs nasi lemak calories
how much rice in nasi padang

confidence_reason: Core nutritional values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo (primary approved source for Singapore dishes) cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD component data. The canonical Singapore hawker nasi padang combination is well-documented in both databases. GI is an analogous estimate based on jasmine rice GI from the University of Sydney GI Database with documented composite meal methodology.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for the composite nasi padang preparation (rendang + sayur lodeh + sambal goreng) is not available in HPB Foodo or IMR Malaysia MyFCD as of 2026-04-13. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite preparation is not reported in the primary sources consulted. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.1g/100g per HPB Foodo source. GI of 62 is an analogous composite estimate; no direct GI measurement of the canonical Singapore nasi padang combination exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load of 46.6 per 400g serving is calculated as (62 × 75g available carbohydrate) / 100. The FoodEx2 code A00PB is the closest parent category; no specific Nasi Padang code exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue. Hindi local name (नासी पदांग) is a transliteration; no established Devanagari-script canonical form exists for this dish in published Indian food databases.
